corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

top_candidates.sentence: By visualizing the first and second layers of Krizhevsky et al. 's architecture (Fig. & )), various problems are apparent.
This new architecture retains much more information in the 1st and 2nd layer features, as shown in Fig. ) & (e).
Large Convolutional Network models have recently demonstrated impressive classification performance on the ImageNet benchmark .
We introduce a novel visualization technique that gives insight into the function of intermediate feature layers and the operation of the classifier.
Used in a diagnostic role, these visualizations allow us to find model architectures that outperform Krizhevsky et al. on the ImageNet classification benchmark.
Used in a diagnostic role, these visualizations allow us to find model architectures that outperform Krizhevsky et al. on the ImageNet classification benchmark.

We show our ImageNet model generalizes well to other datasets: when the softmax classifier is retrained, it convincingly beats the current state-of-the-art results on Caltech-101 and Caltech-256 datasets.

In the last year, several papers have shown that they can also deliver outstanding performance on more challenging visual classification tasks. demonstrate state-of-the-art performance on NORB and CIFAR-10 datasets.
Most notably, show record beating performance on the ImageNet 2012 classification benchmark, with their convnet model achieving an error rate of 16.4%, compared to the 2nd place result of 26.1%.
In this paper we introduce a visualization technique that reveals the input stimuli that excite individual feature maps at any layer in the model.
The visualization technique we propose uses a multi-layered Deconvolutional Network (deconvnet), as proposed by , to project the feature activations back to the input pixel space.
Using these tools, we start with the architecture of and explore different architectures, discovering ones that outperform their results on ImageNet.
Using these tools, we start with the architecture of and explore different architectures, discovering ones that outperform their results on ImageNet.

Our approach, by contrast, provides a non-parametric view of invariance, showing which patterns from the training set activate the feature map. show visualizations that identify patches within a dataset that are responsible for strong activations at higher layers in the model.
Our convnet model generalized less well to the PASCAL data, perhaps suffering from dataset bias , although it was still within 3.2% of the best reported result, despite no tuning for the task.
